question: You are delivering a baby. The head has just delivered. What should you do next before the shoulders deliver?
option_a: Pull on the head to speed delivery
option_b: Check for the umbilical cord around the neck. Run your fingers along the baby's neck to feel for the cord. If present try to slip it over the head. If too tight clamp in two places and cut between before continuing delivery
option_c: Push the head back to slow things down
option_d: Nothing just wait for the shoulders
rationale: After head delivers immediately check for nuchal cord around the neck. This occurs in about 25% of deliveries. If loose slip over the head. If tight it must be clamped and cut to prevent strangulation as the body delivers.

question: The baby has delivered but is limp blue and not breathing. After drying and stimulating for 30 seconds there is no improvement. Heart rate is 80. What should you do?
option_a: The baby just needs more time to wake up
option_b: Begin positive pressure ventilation with BVM. Heart rate below 100 after stimulation requires ventilatory support. Provide effective ventilations at 40-60 per minute. Reassess HR after 30 seconds. If HR below 60 despite ventilation begin compressions
option_c: Start chest compressions immediately
option_d: Give the baby to the mother to warm up
rationale: Newborn HR below 100 after warming drying and stimulating requires positive pressure ventilation. Ventilate at 40-60 breaths per minute with bag-mask. Reassess after 30 seconds. CPR begins if HR remains below 60 despite effective ventilation.

question: After providing ventilations for 30 seconds the newborn's heart rate is now 50 and not improving. What should you do now?
option_a: Continue ventilations only
option_b: Begin chest compressions coordinated with ventilations at a 3:1 ratio. HR below 60 despite effective ventilation requires CPR. Use two-thumb technique with hands encircling chest. Compress one-third chest depth. Continue until HR above 60
option_c: Stop because the baby cannot be saved
option_d: Give oxygen only without compressions
rationale: Newborn with HR persistently below 60 despite adequate ventilation needs chest compressions. Use 3:1 ratio with 90 compressions and 30 breaths per minute. Most newborns respond to effective ventilation so ensure ventilations are producing chest rise.

question: You deliver a healthy baby who cries immediately and is pink. The mother is bleeding heavily from the vagina. The placenta has not yet delivered. What should you do about the bleeding?
option_a: Pull on the cord to deliver the placenta faster
option_b: Do NOT pull on the cord. Allow placenta to deliver naturally. Massage the uterine fundus to promote contraction. Have baby breastfeed if possible. Monitor bleeding closely. If bleeding is excessive treat mother for shock and transport urgently
option_c: The bleeding will stop when the placenta delivers
option_d: Pack the vagina with gauze
rationale: Never pull on the umbilical cord as this can cause cord avulsion or uterine inversion. Uterine massage stimulates contraction to control bleeding. Breastfeeding releases oxytocin which also promotes contraction. Monitor for shock if bleeding is excessive.

question: You notice the umbilical cord is delivering before the baby with the baby still high in the birth canal. What is this emergency and what should you do?
option_a: Normal variation in delivery sequence
option_b: Prolapsed umbilical cord which is an emergency. Insert your gloved hand and push the presenting part up off the cord to relieve compression. Keep pressure off the cord and the cord moist. Transport immediately with mother in knee-chest position for emergency cesarean
option_c: Wait for cord to retract on its own
option_d: Clamp and cut the cord
rationale: Cord prolapse is immediately life-threatening as the cord is compressed cutting off fetal blood supply. Insert hand and lift presenting part off cord. Keep cord moist. Position mother knee-chest or Trendelenburg to reduce pressure. Emergency cesarean is needed.

question: During delivery you see a foot presenting first instead of the head. The foot is now out up to the ankle but delivery is not progressing. What type of presentation is this and what should you do?
option_a: Normal delivery that just needs more time
option_b: Breech presentation with potential for complications. Support what has delivered without pulling. Transport immediately as this requires hospital delivery possibly cesarean. Do not attempt to pull the baby out as head can become trapped
option_c: Pull on the foot to complete delivery
option_d: Push the foot back in
rationale: Footling breech has high complication risk including cord prolapse and head entrapment. Support the presenting part and transport immediately. Do not pull as this can trap the head. Hospital delivery with possible cesarean is needed.

question: The baby's head delivers but the shoulders appear stuck. The mother is pushing but no progress is being made. What is this complication?
option_a: The baby is too big and cannot deliver
option_b: Shoulder dystocia. The anterior shoulder is stuck behind the pubic bone. Have the mother pull her knees sharply toward her chest McRoberts maneuver. Apply suprapubic pressure. Do not push on the uterus or pull hard on the head. This is an emergency
option_c: The mother just needs to push harder
option_d: Wait patiently for natural delivery
rationale: Shoulder dystocia is an emergency with the shoulder stuck behind the pubic bone. McRoberts maneuver sharp hip flexion changes the pelvic angle. Apply suprapubic not fundal pressure. Do not pull hard on the head as this can cause nerve injury.

question: You deliver a baby whose amniotic fluid is thick and greenish-brown. The baby has meconium on the skin and in the mouth. She is limp and not crying. What should you do?
option_a: Stimulate her vigorously to make her cry
option_b: Suction the mouth and nose before stimulating to reduce meconium aspiration risk. If she is not vigorous after clearing the airway provide positive pressure ventilation. Meconium aspiration can cause severe respiratory distress
option_c: The meconium is not a concern
option_d: Wait for her to start breathing on her own
rationale: Thick meconium with a non-vigorous baby requires suctioning before stimulation to reduce aspiration risk. If baby is not responding after clearing airway provide PPV. Meconium aspiration causes severe pneumonitis and respiratory failure.

question: A woman delivers twins. The first baby is healthy. The second baby delivers 10 minutes later but is smaller limp and blue with HR of 70. What should you do?
option_a: Twins usually have different APGAR scores. This is normal
option_b: Begin resuscitation of the second twin immediately. The second twin is in distress with HR below 100 and needs intervention. Each baby needs individual assessment and treatment. Provide PPV and continue until improvement or ALS arrives
option_c: Focus on the healthy twin
option_d: Wait to see if the second twin improves
rationale: Each baby in a multiple birth needs individual assessment. The second twin is showing signs of distress with low HR and cyanosis. Begin appropriate resuscitation. Being a twin does not change resuscitation guidelines.

question: A full-term newborn is one minute old. She has a strong cry flexed extremities pink body with blue hands and feet grimaces with stimulation and HR of 140. What is her APGAR score?
option_b: 8. Two points each for HR above 100 strong cry good tone and active grimace. One point for acrocyanosis blue extremities which is normal initially. APGAR of 7-10 indicates a healthy newborn
rationale: APGAR scoring: Activity 2 good tone. Pulse 2 above 100. Grimace 2 active grimace. Appearance 1 blue extremities pink body. Respirations 2 strong cry. Total is 8. Acrocyanosis blue hands and feet is normal in first minutes.

question: You respond to a patient with Down syndrome who has abdominal pain. His caregiver says he cannot always communicate his symptoms well. How should you approach assessment?
option_a: Talk only to the caregiver since patient cannot communicate
option_b: Address the patient directly first. Use simple language and allow extra time for responses. The caregiver can provide additional information about baseline status and changes. Do not assume the patient cannot understand or participate in their care
option_c: Assume his symptoms are not real
option_d: Use the same approach as any other patient without modification
rationale: Patients with developmental disabilities deserve direct respectful communication. Use simple clear language. Allow extra time. Caregivers provide valuable baseline information. Do not dismiss symptoms because communication is challenging. Adapt your approach while maintaining respect.

question: A deaf patient is having chest pain. She uses sign language but you do not know any signs. How can you communicate effectively?
option_a: Write notes and use gestures. Show her equipment before using it. Use a medical translation app if available. Maintain eye contact when speaking for lip reading. Ensure she understands critical information through written confirmation
option_b: Just talk normally she can probably hear some
option_c: You cannot help her due to the language barrier
option_d: Only talk to family members present
rationale: Written communication gestures and apps help bridge language barriers. Face the patient when speaking. Confirm understanding of critical information in writing. Basic universal gestures can convey pain scales and other assessments.

question: A 400-pound patient needs to be transported for respiratory distress. Your standard stretcher has a 500-pound weight limit. What considerations are important?
option_a: Load him quickly before the stretcher breaks
option_b: Verify weight limits. Request additional personnel for safe lifting and moving. Use proper body mechanics. Ensure ambulance can accommodate size. A bariatric stretcher may be needed if weight exceeds limits. Patient safety and crew safety are both priorities
option_c: Standard equipment works for all patients
option_d: Refuse to transport due to weight
rationale: Bariatric patients require additional planning. Verify equipment weight limits request adequate personnel for safe lifting and ensure vehicle can accommodate the patient. Crew injuries from improper lifting are common. Both patient and provider safety matter.

question: You respond to a patient with a tracheostomy who is in respiratory distress. You hear gurgling from the tracheostomy and she cannot clear it by coughing. What should you do?
option_a: Provide oxygen by face mask
option_b: Suction the tracheostomy to clear secretions. Mucus plugging is common in tracheostomy patients. Use a suction catheter through the stoma. If she has a speaking valve or cap that may need removal. After suctioning provide oxygen through the stoma
option_c: Tell her to cough harder
option_d: Cover the stoma and ventilate by mouth
rationale: Tracheostomy patients cannot clear secretions normally. Suction through the stoma to clear mucus plugging. Remove any speaking valves or caps that might obstruct airflow. Provide oxygen and ventilation through the stoma not the mouth and nose.

question: A home ventilator patient is in distress because his ventilator alarmed and stopped working. He is awake and anxious but still breathing some on his own. His mother is panicked. What should you do first?
option_a: Try to fix the ventilator
option_b: Disconnect from the malfunctioning ventilator and begin manual ventilation with BVM while assessing the patient. Address the immediate respiratory needs first then troubleshoot or arrange backup. Calm reassurance helps reduce anxiety and oxygen demand
option_c: Wait for a respiratory therapist
option_d: Call the ventilator company for phone support
rationale: When a ventilator fails address breathing first. Disconnect and provide manual ventilation with BVM. Assess the patient's condition. The machine problem is secondary to ensuring adequate ventilation. Transport with continued BVM support.

question: A hospice patient with terminal cancer is in severe pain and respiratory distress. She has a DNR order. Her family called 911 because they did not know what else to do. What care can you provide?
option_a: You cannot help hospice patients
option_b: Provide comfort care and symptom management. DNR does not mean no treatment. Position for comfort provide oxygen for dyspnea and support the family. Contact hospice if possible. You can still provide significant help even with DNR status
option_c: Just leave since she has a DNR
option_d: Ignore the DNR and resuscitate if needed
rationale: DNR means no CPR but comfort care is appropriate. Position for comfort provide oxygen for respiratory distress and support the family. Contact hospice for guidance. Hospice patients still benefit from symptom management and compassionate care.

question: A 17-year-old female was found by her mother with superficial cuts on her wrists. She says she does not want to live anymore. She is calm now and says she is fine and will not do it again. Her mother wants her to go to the hospital. Can she refuse?
option_a: No. She needs emergency psychiatric evaluation. Suicidal statements and self-harm require assessment regardless of current calm appearance. A minor making suicidal statements cannot refuse emergency evaluation. Transport for psychiatric assessment
option_b: Yes if she feels fine now she can stay home
option_c: Only if the cuts do not need stitches
option_d: Wait to see if she does it again
rationale: A suicidal minor cannot refuse emergency psychiatric evaluation. Self-harm with suicidal statements requires assessment regardless of current appearance. Transport for psychiatric evaluation. Remove access to means of harm during transport.

question: You respond to an agitated patient who is yelling and pacing. He believes people are trying to kill him and is paranoid of you. He has no weapons visible. How should you approach this situation?
option_a: Rush in and restrain him before he hurts someone
option_b: Maintain safe distance and keep exit route clear. Speak calmly and non-threateningly. Do not argue with his delusions but do not agree with them either. Try to establish rapport. Have law enforcement present. Only restrain if necessary for safety
option_c: Tell him his beliefs are not real
option_d: Leave since he is too dangerous
rationale: Safety first with agitated patients. Maintain distance and escape route. Calm non-confrontational approach works best. Do not argue with or reinforce delusions. Build rapport. Physical restraint is last resort after verbal de-escalation fails.

question: A patient who attempted suicide by overdose vomited and now appears stable. He says he changed his mind and does not want to go to the hospital. He is 35 years old and alert. Can he refuse transport?
option_a: Yes alert adults can refuse any treatment
option_b: No. A patient who just attempted suicide lacks capacity to refuse emergency care regardless of current mental status. Suicidal patients need psychiatric evaluation. The attempt demonstrates impaired judgment. Transport for both medical and psychiatric evaluation
option_c: Only if a family member agrees to watch him
option_d: Yes but get a refusal signature first
rationale: Patients who have just attempted suicide cannot be considered to have decision-making capacity for refusing care. The suicide attempt itself demonstrates impaired judgment. Transport for medical clearance and psychiatric evaluation is necessary.

question: A patient from a culture you are unfamiliar with refuses to let you a male EMR examine his wife. He is not being rude but seems genuinely concerned about modesty. What is the best approach?
option_a: Insist on examining her because it is a medical emergency
option_b: Ask if a female provider can perform the examination. If her condition permits respect cultural preferences while ensuring necessary care is provided. Explain what needs to be done and why. Find ways to maintain modesty while providing care
option_c: Document refusal and leave
option_d: Ignore his concerns and proceed
rationale: Cultural sensitivity improves patient cooperation and outcomes. If possible accommodate preferences such as same-gender providers. Explain medical necessity clearly. Find compromises that provide care while respecting cultural values. Emergency care takes priority but accommodation when possible builds trust.

question: A patient who speaks only Spanish is having chest pain. No interpreter is available. How can you assess and communicate?
option_a: You cannot provide care without an interpreter
option_b: Use simple words gestures and pointing to communicate. Translation apps can help with basic communication. Point to body areas asking about pain. Use OPQRST with gestures. Non-verbal assessment vital signs pulse oximetry provides objective data. Family may help interpret
option_c: Wait for someone who speaks Spanish
option_d: Document language barrier and provide no care
rationale: Language barriers require creative communication. Gestures pointing and translation apps help. Non-verbal assessment provides important information. Family can assist but may not interpret medical terms accurately. Never refuse care due to language barriers.

question: You respond to a homeless man with an infected wound on his leg. He has not been able to keep it clean. He is reluctant to go to the hospital because of past negative experiences with healthcare. How do you encourage him?
option_a: Tell him he will lose his leg if he does not go
option_b: Listen to his concerns without judgment. Explain the medical situation clearly and what will happen at the hospital. Acknowledge past negative experiences while emphasizing the importance of treatment. Treat him with dignity and respect
option_c: Force him to go for his own good
option_d: He is making his own choice so leave
rationale: Building trust through respectful non-judgmental communication is key. Acknowledge concerns listen to past experiences and explain medical needs clearly. Homeless patients often have valid reasons for healthcare reluctance. Dignity and respect improve willingness to accept care.

question: A 10-year-old with a ventriculoperitoneal VP shunt has severe headache vomiting and lethargy. His parents say his shunt sometimes malfunctions. What should you suspect and do?
option_a: Give him medication for headache
option_b: Suspect VP shunt malfunction causing increased intracranial pressure. Symptoms of headache vomiting and lethargy indicate the shunt may be blocked and CSF is not draining properly. Transport for evaluation. This can rapidly progress to herniation if not treated
option_c: This is just a migraine headache
option_d: VP shunts never malfunction
rationale: VP shunt malfunction causes CSF accumulation and increased ICP. Headache vomiting and lethargy are classic signs. This is a medical emergency as ICP can continue rising causing herniation. Transport for shunt evaluation and possible revision.

question: A child with a gastrostomy feeding tube has it accidentally pulled out. The stoma is open but not bleeding significantly. Parents are concerned. What should you advise?
option_a: The stoma will close on its own. No emergency
option_b: Cover the stoma with clean gauze and transport for reinsertion. G-tube stomas can begin to close within hours. While not immediately life-threatening this needs timely reinsertion. Do not attempt to reinsert a tube yourself. Keep the site clean
option_c: Reinsert the tube yourself
option_d: Wait a day to see if it needs to be replaced
rationale: G-tube stomas can close within hours if the tube is not replaced. Cover with clean dressing to prevent contamination and transport for proper reinsertion. Do not attempt to reinsert as you may create a false passage.

question: A 75-year-old woman with dementia is your patient. She cannot tell you what is wrong but her caregiver says she has been refusing to eat and is more confused than usual. Temperature is 99.8°F. Her caregiver knows her baseline and is concerned something is wrong. What is your assessment?
option_a: She is just having a bad day with her dementia
option_b: Take the caregiver's concerns seriously. Change from baseline increased confusion and refusing to eat in an elderly patient with low-grade fever suggests infection possibly UTI. Elderly often present atypically. Transport for evaluation
option_c: Dementia patients cannot provide useful information
option_d: Low-grade fever is not concerning
rationale: Caregivers know their patients baseline. Change from baseline is significant even without specific complaints. Elderly patients especially with dementia present atypically for infections. UTI commonly causes confusion without typical urinary symptoms. Evaluate based on change from normal.
